message.content:
Rewrite the file at C:\Users\Charlie\.claude\skills\minimax-dev-workflow.md with the complete new content below.

The new version must:
1. Be a proper SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter (name, description, compatibility, triggers)
2. Include a Pre-Task step: "Read _learnings.md and apply relevant learnings"
3. Include a Post-Task step: "Log session metrics to _learnings.md, then extract 3-5 new learnings"
4. The description in frontmatter must include specific trigger phrases

Write this exact content to the file:

```markdown
---
name: minimax-dev-workflow
description: >
  Full-stack development using MiniMax agents with self-improvement built in.
  TRIGGERS: "review my code", "fix the bugs", "refactor X", "audit Y",
  "add tests to Z", "/code-review", "/refactor", "full code review".
  BEFORE EVERY TASK: read C:\Users\Charlie\.claude\skills\_learnings.md
  AFTER EVERY TASK: log session metrics and extract learnings.
  Composes with: minimax-delegation, skill-builder, token-budget-controller.
compatibility: [file-read, file-write, agent-spawn]
---

# minimax-dev-workflow

Self-improving MiniMax agent workflow for code review, bug fixes, refactors,
and multi-step development tasks. Reads _learnings.md before every task.
Writes learnings after every session. Sonnet orchestrates; MiniMax executes.

## Core Principle: Read Learnings First

**MANDATORY**: Before every task, read C:\Users\Charlie\.claude\skills\_learnings.md
Apply any relevant learnings from past sessions. Never repeat the same mistake twice.

## Core Principle: Pre-Read Once

Read each source file EXACTLY ONCE. Paste file content directly into agent prompts.
Never let an agent re-read a file you've already read this session.
Why: 20k-token file × 12 re-reads = 240k wasted tokens. Pre-read once = ~80% token savings.

## When to Use

- Code review of a project or directory
- Fixing bugs across multiple files
- Refactoring a codebase
- Adding test coverage
- Security audits
- Any multi-step development task

## When NOT to Use

- Single-file, single-fix: spawn one MiniMax agent directly instead
- Research-only tasks: use minimax-delegation for simple spawning

## Workflow

### Step 1 — Read Learnings (MANDATORY)

Read C:\Users\Charlie\.claude\skills\_learnings.md
Apply these rules to every decision:
- GROUP all fixes for the same file into ONE agent (never multiple agents on same file)
- PRE-READ files once, paste content into prompts (no re-reads by agents)
- TARGET: 2-5 agents per project regardless of issue count
- LOG all session metrics (tokens, duration, issues fixed)

### Step 2 — Explore Files (Sonnet does this)

Read ALL relevant files ONCE. Build an issue map:
- What files exist and what are their logical units?
- What issues need addressing in each file?
- Are there CLAUDE.md or project rules to reference?

Do NOT spawn agents yet.

### Step 3 — Plan Dispatch

Group by file: 1 agent per file. Never 2 agents on the same file.

| Grouping | Target agents |
|----------|--------------|
| 1-3 related files | 1 agent |
| 4-10 files | 2-3 agents |
| 10+ files | 4-5 agents (cap) |

### Step 4 — Pre-Read Files (Sonnet does this)

Paste full file content into each agent's prompt. No re-reads.

For files >5k tokens: split into logical sections with clear markers.

### Step 5 — Spawn Parallel Agents

Use minimax-delegation patterns. Spawn ALL agents simultaneously.
Track dispatch in your response so the user can see what's running.

### Step 6 — Verify and Synthesize

When agents complete:
1. Confirm syntax: `python -m py_compile` on every edited file
2. Report: what was done per file
3. Note any issues agents couldn't resolve

### Step 7 — Log Session (MANDATORY)

After all agents complete, record session to _learnings.md:
```
## Session: YYYY-MM-DD — [Task Name]
- Files: [list]
- Issues fixed: [count]
- Agents spawned: [count]
- Tokens: [from notifications]
- Duration: [from notifications]

### What worked
- [specific technique]

### What failed
- [specific mistake]
- [better alternative]

### Actionable improvement
- [one specific change for next time]
```

Then spawn a MiniMax learning agent to extract 3-5 new learnings from this session
and append them to C:\Users\Charlie\.claude\skills\_learnings.md

## Agent Prompt Template

```
You are a MiniMax development agent.

## Context
[Paste relevant learnings from _learnings.md here]

## Project Rules
[Paste CLAUDE.md or relevant project rules]

## Your Task
[Specific task description]

## Files to Edit

### File: [path]
```[language]
[paste full file content here]
```

## Issues to Address

Prioritized by severity. Address all CRITICAL and HIGH first.
1. [CRITICAL] [issue + exact fix]
2. [HIGH] [issue + exact fix]
3. [MEDIUM] [issue + fix if straightforward]

## Rules
- Immutability: never mutate existing objects
- Error handling: no bare except:, no silent pass
- Type hints: required on all function signatures
- No hardcoded secrets
- After changes: `python -m py_compile [file]`
- If you cannot complete: describe why, continue to next issue

## Verify Before Finishing
Run `python -m py_compile [file]` for every edited file. Report clean/dirty per file.
```

## Important Rules

1. **No merge conflicts**: 1 agent per file
2. **No redundant reads**: paste content into prompt, agent does not re-read
3. **Max 5 simultaneous agents**: beyond this, runtime overhead exceeds parallelism benefit
4. **Fail gracefully**: note blockers, continue to next issue
5. **Log everything**: token counts, duration, issues resolved — all go to _learnings.md

## Spawn Patterns

From minimax-delegation:
- PARALLEL: independent files → Agent() × N
- HYBRID: Phase 1 (parallel discovery) → Phase 2 (parallel fixes)

## Output Format

Always end with:
| File | Issues | Status |
|------|--------|--------|
| file_a.py | 3 CRITICAL, 2 HIGH | ✅ Done |
| file_b.py | 1 HIGH | ✅ Done |
| file_c.py | 1 CRITICAL | ⚠️ Skipped — reason |
```

content.content:
1	---
2	name: minimax-dev-workflow
3	description: >
4	  Full-stack development workflow using MiniMax agents for all execution.
5	  Use whenever the user wants to: code review a project, fix bugs, refactor code,
6	  add tests, audit security, or do any multi-step development task.
7	  Triggers on: "review my code", "fix the bugs", "refactor X", "audit Y",
8	  "add tests to Z", "/code-review", "/refactor", "run a full review".
9	  Composes with: minimax-delegation (raw spawning patterns), skill-builder (for
10	  creating new workflow skills). Sonnet stays in orchestration/synthesis only.
11	compatibility: [file-read, file-write, agent-spawn]
12	---
13	
14	# minimax-dev-workflow
15	
16	Full development workflow: explore once → dispatch by file → synthesize results.
17	All execution goes through MiniMax agents. Sonnet orchestrates, never codes.
18	
19	## Core Principle: Pre-Read Once
20	
21	**This is the #1 efficiency rule.** Each source file should be read exactly once in a
22	session. Never let a sub-agent re-read a file you've already read.
23	
24	Why: A 20k-token file read 12 times = 240k tokens wasted. Read once, pass content
25	directly to agents = ~80% token reduction. The agents are cheap; the redundant I/O is not.
26	
27	## When to Use
28	
29	- Code review of a project or directory
30	- Fixing bugs across multiple files
31	- Refactoring a codebase
32	- Adding test coverage
33	- Security audits
34	- Any multi-step development task
35	
36	## When NOT to Use
37	
38	- Single-file, single-fix tasks (spawn one MiniMax agent directly instead)
39	- One-shot questions (just answer it)
40	- Research-only tasks (use minimax-delegation for simple spawning)
41	
42	## Workflow
43	
44	### Step 1 — Explore (Sonnet does this)
45	
46	Read ALL relevant files ONCE. Build an in-memory issue/structure map:
47	
48	```
49	For each target file:
50	  - What is it? (module, config, test, etc.)
51	  - Rough line count and complexity
52	  - Key issues visible at a glance
53	  - Are there CLAUDE.md / rules / docs?
54	
55	Group files by logical unit:
56	  - File A + File B are tightly coupled → same agent
57	  - File C is independent → separate agent
58	```
59	
60	Do NOT spawn agents yet. You are mapping the terrain first.
61	
62	### Step 2 — Plan the Dispatch
63	
64	Group issues by two dimensions:
65	
66	| Dimension | Rule |
67	|-----------|------|
68	| **File** | One agent per file or logical unit. Never two agents editing the same file. |
69	| **Severity** | CRITICAL/HIGH first, MEDIUM second, LOW skip unless trivial. |
70	| **Independence** | Independent files → parallel. Dependent tasks → sequential or hybrid. |
71	
72	Target: **1 MiniMax agent per file**, not 1 agent per issue.
73	
74	### Step 3 — Pre-Read Files (Sonnet does this)
75	
76	For each agent dispatch, paste the file content directly into the prompt.
77	Do NOT rely on the agent to read the file — they've already got it.
78	
79	For files >5k tokens: split into logical sections with clear markers.
80	
81	### Step 4 — Spawn Parallel Agents
82	
83	Use `minimax-delegation` patterns here. Spawn ALL agents simultaneously.
84	
85	Track what was dispatched in your response so the user can see the full picture.
86	
87	### Step 5 — Aggregate and Report
88	
89	When agents complete:
90	1. What was done, per file
91	2. Any issues that couldn't be resolved
92	3. Final status table
93	
94	## Agent Prompt Template
95	
96	Use this template for each spawned agent. Fill in the blanks.
97	
98	```
99	You are a MiniMax development agent.
100	
101	## Your Task
102	[Task description — be specific about what to do and what to avoid]
103	
104	## Project Context
105	[Paste CLAUDE.md or relevant project rules here if present]
106	
107	## Files to Edit
108	
109	### File: [relative path]
110	```python
111	[paste full file content here — paste entire file, not just the relevant section]
112	```
113	
114	## Issues to Address
115	
116	For this file, address these in order:
117	1. [CRITICAL] [description of the issue and exact fix]
118	2. [HIGH] [description of the issue and exact fix]
119	3. [MEDIUM] [description — only fix if straightforward]
120	
121	## Rules
122	
123	- Immutability: never mutate existing objects, return new copies
124	- Error handling: no bare `except:`, no silent `pass`
125	- Type hints: required on all function signatures
126	- No hardcoded secrets — use environment variables
127	- After making changes, verify syntax: `python -m py_compile [file]`
128	- If you cannot complete an issue, describe why and continue to the next
129	
130	## Verify Before Finishing
131	
132	Run `python -m py_compile [file]` for every file you edited.
133	Report whether each file compiles cleanly.
134	```
135	
136	## Important Rules
137	
138	1. **No merge conflicts**: One agent per file. If two issues need the same file, they
139	   go to the same agent.
140	2. **No redundant reads**: If you've already read a file in this session, paste it
141	   into the agent prompt. Never say "read the file" — the agent will re-read it.
142	3. **Max parallelism**: Keep 3–10 simultaneous agents. Beyond 10, the runtime
143	   overhead exceeds the parallelism benefit.
144	4. **Fail gracefully**: If an agent can't complete, note it and move on. Don't
145	   block other agents.
146	5. **Verify syntax**: Every agent should run `python -m py_compile` on every file
147	   they edit before reporting done.
148	
149	## Spawning Patterns
150	
151	Use these from `minimax-delegation`:
152	- **PARALLEL**: Multiple independent files → `Agent()` × N simultaneously
153	- **HYBRID**: Phase 1 (parallel research/discovery) → Phase 2 (parallel fixes)
154	
155	```
156	// HYBRID example
157	Agent(r1, {prompt: "Review file A, list issues"})
158	Agent(r2, {prompt: "Review file B, list issues"})
159	// Wait for both, then:
160	Agent(f1, {prompt: "Fix all issues in file A. Content: [paste file A]"})
161	Agent(f2, {prompt: "Fix all issues in file B. Content: [paste file B]"})
162	```
163	
164	## Relationship to minimax-delegation
165	
166	`minimax-delegation` = raw spawning patterns (single, parallel, sequential, hybrid).
167	`minimax-dev-workflow` = full workflow with the pre-read-once efficiency layer.
168	
169	Use `minimax-delegation` for: "just spawn X agents to do Y."
170	Use `minimax-dev-workflow` for: "do a full code review + fix cycle on this project."
171	
172	## Output Format
173	
174	Always end with a status table:
175	
176	| File | Issues Fixed | Status |
177	|------|-------------|--------|
178	| file_a.py | 3 CRITICAL, 2 HIGH | ✅ Done |
179	| file_b.py | 1 HIGH | ✅ Done |
180	| file_c.py | 1 CRITICAL | ⚠️ Skipped — [reason] |
181
